perturbation.original_text: Payments due under Section 2.2 shall be payable to PcoMed by Integra on a quarterly basis within 45 days following the end of each calendar quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments due under Section 2.2 shall be payable to PcoMed by Integra on a quarterly basis within 90 days following the end of each calendar quarter.
perturbation.explanation: The payment term has been extended to 90 days, which might violate prompt payment laws in certain jurisdictions if they mandate faster payment for services rendered. This creates a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.J. Stat. § 2A:30A-2 requires payments to contractors (PcoMed) to be made promptly according to the contract terms. Extending the payment term to 90 days without a clear justification could be challenged under this act if it's deemed unreasonable or causes undue hardship.

(here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 2A - Administration of Civil and Criminal Justice Section 2A:30A-2 - Payment to prime contractor, subcontractor, subsubcontractor, timely payment; exceptions; disputes; resolution. Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 2A:30A-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 2A:30A-2 Payment to prime contractor, subcontractor, subsubcontractor, timely payment; exceptions; disputes; resolution. 2. a. If a prime contractor has performed in accordance with the provisions of a contract with the owner and the billing for the work has been approved and certified by the owner or the owner's authorized approving agent, the owner shall pay the amount due to the prime contractor for each periodic payment, final payment or retainage monies not more than 30 calendar days after the billing date, which for a periodic billing, shall be the periodic billing date specified in the contract. The billing shall be deemed approved and certified 20 days after the owner receives it unless the owner provides, before the end of the 20-day period, a written statement of the amount withheld and the reason for withholding payment, except that in the case of a public or governmental entity that requires the entity's governing body to vote on authorizations for each periodic payment, final payment or retainage monies, the amount due may be approved and certified at the next scheduled public meeting of the entity's governing body, and paid during the entity's subsequent payment cycle, provided this exception has been defined in the bid specifications and contract documents. b. If a subcontractor or subsubcontractor has performed in accordance with the provisions of its contract with the prime contractor or subcontractor and the work has been accepted by the owner, the owner's authorized approving agent, or the prime contractor, as applicable, and the parties have not otherwise agreed in writing, the prime contractor shall pay to its subcontractor and the subcontractor shall pay to its subsubcontractor within 10 calendar days of the receipt of each periodic payment, final payment or receipt of retainage monies, the full amount received for the work of the subcontractor or subsubcontractor based on the work completed or the services rendered under the applicable contract. In the case of ongoing work on the same project for which partial payments are made, the amount of money owed for work already completed shall only be payable if the subcontractor or subsubcontractor is performing to the satisfaction of the prime contractor or subcontractor, as applicable. c. If a payment due pursuant to the provisions of this section is not made in a timely manner, the delinquent party shall be liable for the amount of money owed under the contract, plus
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment term from 45 to 90 days, which contradicts the prompt payment requirement outlined in N.J. Stat. § 2A:30A-2. The law snippet specifies that an owner shall pay the prime contractor within 30 calendar days after the billing date. While the contract is between PcoMed and Integra, not an owner and prime contractor, the general principle of timely payment is undermined by extending the payment term significantly beyond the legally suggested timeframe, particularly when no justification is provided. This extension creates a legal contradiction as it deviates from the prompt payment standard implied by the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed to in writing by the parties, each party agrees that, for the term of this Agreement and for 20 years thereafter, it shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement any Confidential Information furnished to it by the other party pursuant to this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed to in writing by the parties, each party agrees that, for the term of this Agreement and for 1 year thereafter, it shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement any Confidential Information furnished to it by the other party pursuant to this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 1 year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable state laws, which often allow for longer protection periods if the information remains a trade secret. This creates a contradiction as trade secret protection can last indefinitely as long as secrecy is maintained.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the New Jersey Trade Secrets Act, trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may expose trade secrets to disclosure, effectively nullifying their protection under the Act, which has no fixed expiration.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from 20 years to 1 year, which contradicts the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. The Law Explanation states that reducing the confidentiality period to one year may expose trade secrets, nullifying their protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that the 'New Jersey Trade Secrets Act' exists. The change creates a significant risk of exposing trade secrets prematurely, conflicting with the law's intent to protect trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any controversy or claim relating to, arising out of or in any way connected to any provision of this Agreement (a "Dispute"), either Party may, by notice to the other Party, have such dispute referred to their respective senior officials designated below or their successors, for attempted resolution by good faith negotiations within 30 days after such notice is received. Any Dispute that is not resolved through such negotiations may be referred to binding arbitration in Denver, Colorado with the Judicial Arbiter Group as part of a 3 person panel, with costs borne separately by each party, to be conducted in accordance with the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any controversy or claim relating to, arising out of or in any way connected to any provision of this Agreement (a "Dispute"), the Parties agree to resolve the matter through mandatory binding arbitration in Denver, Colorado. Any dispute not resolved within 10 days of written notice shall proceed directly to arbitration, waiving any preliminary negotiation or mediation.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the dispute resolution timeframe to 10 days and eliminating preliminary negotiation may conflict with good faith requirements under contract law. Some jurisdictions require a reasonable opportunity to resolve disputes before arbitration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law and Good Faith Negotiation
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Eliminating a reasonable negotiation period and mandating arbitration within 10 days may be seen as a violation of this duty, particularly if it prejudices one party's ability to adequately prepare their case or explore alternative resolutions. While not a direct statute, it reflects a widely recognized legal principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates arbitration within 10 days, eliminating preliminary negotiation, potentially conflicting with the 'obligation of good faith' in contract performance, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards.' Snippet 2, referencing the Uniform Commercial Code, states that 'Every contract or duty... imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The shortened timeframe and elimination of negotiation may not meet the 'fair dealing standards' or 'good faith' requirements, creating a contradiction.
